_comment: Curated illustrative examples for the KYC / beneficial-ownership use-case core concepts. Real-world entity-resolution instances (general domain knowledge), not FIBO axioms. Applied by etl/to_okf.py when FIBO supplies none; provenance curated.

examples.title: party
examples: A person or organization that is party to a contract, such as the customer opening an account.

examples.title: agent
examples: A party that can act, such as a company acting through its officers to sign a contract.

examples.title: person
examples: An individual human being, e.g. Jane Doe, born 12 March 1985.

examples.title: legally competent natural person
examples: An adult of sound mind able to enter a contract, e.g. an 18-year-old account applicant.

examples.title: customer
examples: An individual or company that holds an account with, or buys services from, a bank.

examples.title: client
examples: A company that engages an investment adviser to manage its portfolio.

examples.title: contract party
examples: The borrower and the lender named on a loan agreement.

examples.title: person name
examples: 'Jane Elizabeth Doe', the legal name recorded on a customer's identity document.

examples.title: account holder
examples: The named owner of a checking account, e.g. Jane Doe on account 000123456.

examples.title: customer account
examples: A retail checking account held by an individual at a bank.

examples.title: customer account holder
examples: The customer named as the holder of a deposit account.

examples.title: legal person
examples: Acme Corporation, an entity that can own assets and enter contracts in its own name.

examples.title: corporation
examples: Apple Inc., a corporation registered in California.

examples.title: for profit corporation
examples: A privately held manufacturing company organized to earn profit for its shareholders.

examples.title: not-for-profit corporation
examples: A charitable hospital incorporated as a non-profit.

examples.title: LEI registered entity
examples: A fund that has obtained a Legal Entity Identifier in order to trade derivatives.

examples.title: non-governmental organization
examples: The International Committee of the Red Cross.

examples.title: not for profit organization
examples: A community foundation that reinvests any surplus in its mission.

examples.title: partnership
examples: A two-partner law firm, Smith & Jones LLP.

examples.title: general partnership
examples: A partnership in which all partners share unlimited liability for the firm's debts.

examples.title: trust
examples: A family trust holding property for the benefit of the settlor's children.

examples.title: living trust
examples: A revocable living trust created to hold a person's assets during their lifetime.

examples.title: irrevocable trust
examples: An irrevocable trust whose terms the settlor cannot change once it is established.

examples.title: ownership
examples: A shareholder's ownership of 100 shares in a company.

examples.title: entity ownership
examples: A parent company's ownership of a wholly owned subsidiary.

examples.title: ownership control situation
examples: An individual owning 40% of a company's shares and also holding board control.

examples.title: beneficial owner
examples: The individual who ultimately owns 30% of a company through a chain of holding companies.

examples.title: beneficial ownership
examples: Ultimate ownership of a company traced through two intermediate holding companies to a person.

examples.title: control
examples: A shareholder's ability to direct the decisions of a company.

examples.title: de facto control
examples: Control exercised in practice, e.g. directing a company despite holding under 50% of its shares.

examples.title: de jure control
examples: Control by legal right, e.g. holding more than 50% of the voting shares.

examples.title: controlling party
examples: The majority shareholder that directs a subsidiary's board.

examples.title: entity controlling party
examples: A holding company that controls its operating subsidiary.

examples.title: controlled party
examples: A subsidiary controlled by its parent company.

examples.title: majority controlling party
examples: A shareholder holding 55% of the voting rights.

examples.title: controlling equity
examples: A 60% equity stake that confers voting control.

examples.title: board composition control
examples: Control through the right to appoint a majority of the board of directors.

examples.title: de jure controlling interest party
examples: A shareholder with a legal majority of the voting rights.

examples.title: contractual control
examples: Control established by a shareholders' agreement rather than by share ownership.

examples.title: legal entity identifier
examples: 5493001KJTIIGC8Y1R12, the 20-character LEI of a specific legal entity (ISO 17442).

examples.title: legal entity identifier scheme
examples: The Global LEI System, operated under GLEIF.

examples.title: entity legal form identifier
examples: An ELF code, such as one identifying the 'private limited company' legal form.

examples.title: business identifier code
examples: BOFAUS3N, the SWIFT BIC for Bank of America.

examples.title: identity document
examples: A passport or national ID card presented at account onboarding.

examples.title: driver's license identifier
examples: The license number on a state-issued driver's license used as identification.

examples.title: customer identifier
examples: The unique customer number a bank assigns at onboarding.

examples.title: client identifier
examples: A reference code identifying a client in an adviser's records.

examples.title: business registration authority
examples: A companies registry such as the UK Companies House.

examples.title: legal entity identifier registry
examples: A GLEIF-accredited Local Operating Unit that issues LEIs.

examples.title: business register identifier
examples: A company registration number issued by a national business registry.

examples.title: registration authority code
examples: An RA code drawn from the ISO 20275 registration-authorities list.
